section.title: Penalties for Sin
section.sectionRationale: This chapter supplies the civil penalties, largely capital, for many of the offenses named in chapters 18 and 19, Molech worship, occult practices, cursing parents, and specific sexual sins, functioning as Israel's old covenant enforcement of the holiness just described.
section.thesis: God attaches severe, largely capital civil penalties to Israel's most serious covenant violations, showing how seriously He regards both fidelity to Him and the purity of family life, while entrusting the actual execution of judgment to Israel's courts as a whole community, not to individuals.
section.frameNote: Three movements: capital penalties for Molech worship and consulting mediums; penalties for cursing parents and a list of sexual offenses paralleling chapter 18; and the closing call to be holy and set apart, since the LORD has set Israel apart from the nations.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Tell the Israelites, ‘Any Israelite or foreigner living in Israel who gives any of his children to Molech must be put to death. The people of the land are to stone him.
And I will set My face against that man and cut him off from his people, because by giving his offspring to Molech, he has defiled My sanctuary and {{chalal|profaned}} My holy name.
And if the people of the land ever hide their eyes and fail to put to death the man who gives one of his children to Molech,
then I will set My face against that man and his family and cut off from among their people both him and all who follow him in prostituting themselves with Molech.
Whoever turns to mediums or spiritists to prostitute himself with them, I will also set My face against that person and cut him off from his people.
Consecrate yourselves, therefore, and be holy, because I am the LORD your God.
And you shall keep My statutes and practice them. I am the LORD who sanctifies you.
If anyone curses his father or mother, he must be put to death. He has cursed his father or mother; his {{dam|blood}} shall be upon him.
If a man commits adultery with another man’s wife, with the wife of his neighbor, both the adulterer and the adulteress must surely be put to death.
If a man lies with his father’s wife, he has uncovered his father’s nakedness. Both must surely be put to death; their blood is upon them.
If a man lies with his daughter-in-law, both must surely be put to death. They have acted perversely; their blood is upon them.
If a man lies with a man as with a woman, they have both committed an {{toevah|abomination}}. They must surely be put to death; their blood is upon them.
If a man marries both a woman and her mother, it is depraved. Both he and they must be burned in the fire, so that there will be no depravity among you.
If a man lies carnally with an animal, he must be put to death. And you are also to kill the animal.
If a woman approaches any animal to mate with it, you must kill both the woman and the animal. They must surely be put to death; their blood is upon them.

If a man marries his sister, whether the daughter of his father or of his mother, and they have sexual relations, it is a disgrace. They must be cut off in the sight of their people. He has uncovered the nakedness of his sister; he shall bear his iniquity.

If a man lies with a menstruating woman and has sexual relations with her, he has exposed the source of her flow, and she has uncovered the source of her blood. Both of them must be cut off from among their people.
You must not have sexual relations with the sister of your mother or your father, for it is exposing one’s own kin; both shall bear their iniquity.
If a man lies with his uncle’s wife, he has uncovered the nakedness of his uncle. They will bear their sin; they shall die childless.
If a man marries his brother’s wife, it is an act of impurity. He has uncovered the nakedness of his brother; they shall be childless.

units.label: Set apart, and holy to the LORD

verses.text: You are therefore to keep all My statutes and ordinances, so that the land where I am bringing you to live will not vomit you out.
You must not follow the statutes of the nations I am driving out before you. Because they did all these things, I abhorred them.

But I have told you that you will inherit their land, since I will give it to you as an inheritance, a land flowing with milk and honey. I am the LORD your God, who has set you apart from the peoples.

You are therefore to distinguish between clean and unclean animals and birds. Do not become contaminated by any animal or bird, or by anything that crawls on the ground; I have set these apart as unclean for you.
You are to be {{qadosh|holy}} to Me because I, the LORD, am holy, and I have set you apart from the nations to be My own.
A man or a woman who is a medium or spiritist must surely be put to death. They shall be stoned; their blood is upon them.’”

chalal.gloss: To profane, defile, or treat as common what should be holy. Applied here to giving a child to Molech, this word marks the offense as an assault not merely on the child but on God's own reputation and holy name.

dam.gloss: Blood. The recurring formula their blood is upon them or his blood is upon him places responsibility for a capital sentence squarely on the guilty party, rather than on the community that carries it out.

toevah.gloss: Abomination, something detestable. Applied here, as in chapter 18, to a specific sexual act, this word carries the same strong moral weight it carries elsewhere in the Old Testament for idolatry and dishonest business practice.

qadosh.gloss: Holy, set apart. The chapter closes as it began in chapter 19, grounding Israel's whole ethical and civil order in the character of the LORD Himself, who has set Israel apart from the nations to belong to Him.

basic.context: This chapter assigns civil penalties, mostly execution by the community, to the offenses named in chapters 18 and 19: child sacrifice to Molech, consulting mediums, cursing parents, adultery, incest, same sex relations, and bestiality.
basic.hermeneutics: This is Israel's old covenant civil and judicial law, tied to its unique status as a theocratic nation under God's direct rule in the land. The New Testament nowhere directs the church or civil governments to enforce these specific penalties; the church's own discipline for comparable sin is exclusion from fellowship aimed at repentance and restoration, not execution, 1 Corinthians 5:1-5, 13.
basic.originalLanguages: The recurring formula their blood is upon them places moral responsibility for the capital sentence on the guilty party rather than the community executing it. Chalal, profane, marks Molech worship as an assault on God's own name; toevah, abomination, applies the same strong term used in chapter 18 to specific sexual sin; qadosh, holy, closes the chapter as it opened the last, grounding everything in God's own character.
basic.typology: The chapter's repeated sentence of death for unrepented sin points toward the judgment Christ bore in His people's place; and Israel's status as a people set apart from the nations anticipates the church, a holy nation, God's own possession, set apart not by land or bloodline but by union with Christ.
basic.christ: Galatians 3:13 says Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us; the severity of this chapter's penalties shows exactly what judgment sin deserves, a judgment Christ bore in the place of all who trust Him, so that the sentence of death this chapter pronounces is, for believers, already fully borne by their substitute.
basic.god: God's holiness demands that covenant violations touching worship and family integrity be treated as matters of life and death for the whole community, not private preference; yet the reason given for holiness is God's own character, not mere fear of penalty, and in Christ deserved judgment gives way to mercy for all who repent.
basic.crossReferences: Galatians 3:13 says Christ became a curse for us to redeem us from the law's curse. 1 Corinthians 5:1-5, 13 shows the church's actual practice with serious sin among believers, exclusion and restoration, not execution. Hebrews 12:24 contrasts the blood that cries for judgment with the blood of Christ, which speaks a better word.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows exactly how seriously God regards sin against Himself and against the family He designed; it prepares the way for the gospel's full weight, since Christ bore precisely the judgment this chapter pronounces, in the place of everyone who turns to Him, so that mercy, not merely punishment, becomes the last word.

context.literary: This chapter functions as the judicial counterpart to chapters 18 and 19, attaching civil penalties to many of the same offenses those chapters describe, and closing the section with the same call to holiness that opened chapter 19.
context.historical: Israel functioned as a theocratic nation under God's direct covenant rule, with its own land, courts, and civil order; these penalties belonged to that specific national structure, carried out by the community as a whole rather than by private individuals.
context.canonical: This chapter's vocabulary of blood, guilt, and being cut off or set apart feeds directly into the New Testament's language of curse, redemption, and the church's own identity as a people set apart in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish decisive, corporate consequences for the specific violations named in chapters 18 and 19, showing that these were not private matters but capital threats to the whole community's covenant relationship with God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes and prescribes the specific civil and judicial penalties of Israel's unique old covenant theocracy, tied to its land and national covenant; these penalties are not directly binding on civil law or church discipline today. First Corinthians 5 shows the New Testament church's own procedure for serious sin among believers is exclusion and restoration, not execution, even though the moral seriousness of the underlying offenses endures.

debates.question: Do the capital penalties in this chapter apply to civil law or church practice today?

views.label: Old covenant theocratic law, not directly binding today
views.case: Israel was a unique covenant nation under God's direct rule, with its own land and civil structure; the New Testament nowhere directs the church or civil governments to enforce these specific penalties, and the church's discipline for comparable sin is exclusion from fellowship aimed at repentance, not execution, 1 Corinthians 5:1-5, 13.

views.label: A witness to the seriousness of these sins even where the specific penalty does not transfer
views.case: Even those who agree the death penalty itself does not carry over recognize that the severity of these penalties testifies to how seriously God regards these violations, informing, without dictating word for word, how church discipline and pastoral care should treat them today.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the church does not execute these penalties today; Christians live under civil governments, not the Mosaic theocracy, and the New Testament redirects serious sin among believers toward church discipline aimed at repentance and restoration, not capital punishment.

study.originalLanguages: The formula their blood is upon them, built on dam, blood, repeatedly places responsibility for the capital sentence on the guilty party rather than on the community carrying it out. Chalal, to profane, marks Molech worship as a direct assault on God's holy name; toevah, abomination, is applied to a specific sexual sin exactly as it was in chapter 18; and qadosh, holy, frames the whole chapter within Israel's calling to reflect God's own character.

typology.type: The repeated sentence, 'their blood is upon them,' marking personal responsibility for capital guilt
typology.fulfillment: Contrasted in the gospel by the blood of Christ, sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel, cleansing rather than condemning those who trust Him, Hebrews 12:24.

typology.type: Israel set apart from the nations as holy
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the church, a holy nation, God's own possession, set apart not by national boundaries but by union with Christ, 1 Peter 2:9-10.

study.christ: Galatians 3:13 declares that Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us. The severity of this chapter's penalties shows precisely the weight of judgment sin deserves before a holy God, judgment Christ bore Himself in the place of everyone who trusts Him, so that the sentence of death this chapter pronounces on unrepented sin has already been fully borne by their substitute.
study.god: God's holiness demands that covenant violations touching worship, family, and sexual integrity be treated by the whole community as matters of life and death, not private preference; yet the reason given for holiness, repeated at the chapter's close, is God's own character, I am the LORD who sanctifies you, not merely the threat of penalty. In Christ, the judgment this chapter describes gives way to mercy for all who repent and believe.

crossReferences.note: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law, becoming a curse for us, bearing the judgment this chapter's penalties describe.
The New Testament church's actual response to serious sin among believers is exclusion aimed at repentance and restoration, not execution.
The blood of Christ speaks a better word than the blood crying for judgment, offering cleansing rather than condemnation.
The church is called a holy nation, God's own possession, set apart in Christ.

study.oneStory: This chapter shows exactly how seriously God regards sin against Himself and against the family He designed, attaching the gravest civil penalty Israel's law knew. That very severity prepares the way for the gospel's full weight, since Christ bore precisely the judgment this chapter pronounces, in the place of everyone who turns to Him, so that mercy, not merely punishment, becomes the last word for all who trust Him.

quiz.q: What penalty is prescribed for giving one's child to Molech?

options.text: Death by stoning at the hands of the community.
options.note: Verse 2 specifies this penalty explicitly.

options.text: A fine paid to the priest.
options.note: The text specifies execution, not a monetary fine.

options.text: Temporary exile from the camp.
options.note: Verse 2 specifies death, not temporary exile.

options.text: No penalty is specified.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly specifies the penalty of death by stoning.

quiz.q: What happens if the community fails to execute the penalty against someone who gives a child to Molech, according to verses 4-5?

options.text: God Himself will set His face against that man and his family and cut them off from among their people.
options.note: Verses 4-5 state this consequence directly.

options.text: The matter is simply forgotten.
options.note: Verses 4-5 explicitly describe God's own judgment in that case.

options.text: The community itself is rewarded for leniency.
options.note: The text describes divine judgment, not reward, for failing to enforce this law.

options.text: No consequence is described.
options.note: Verses 4-5 explicitly describe a consequence.

quiz.q: What recurring phrase closes many of the individual case laws about sexual offenses in this chapter?

options.text: "Their blood is upon them," or "his blood shall be upon him."
options.note: This phrase recurs at verses 9, 11, 12, 13, 16, and 27.

options.text: "They shall be forgiven immediately."
options.note: The recurring phrase places responsibility for guilt on the offender, not immediate forgiveness.

options.text: "No punishment applies to this case."
options.note: The recurring phrase marks these as capital offenses, not cases without punishment.

options.text: No recurring phrase appears in the chapter.
options.note: The phrase recurs multiple times through the chapter.

quiz.q: What reason does God give in verses 22-26 for keeping all these statutes?

options.text: So the land will not vomit Israel out, since God has set them apart from the nations to be holy to Him.
options.note: Verses 22-26 state this reasoning directly, echoing chapter 18's warning.

options.text: So that Israel could gain military alliances with neighboring nations.
options.note: The stated reason concerns holiness and the land, not foreign alliances.

options.text: No reason is given for keeping these statutes.
options.note: Verses 22-26 give this reasoning explicitly.

options.text: So that Israel would become identical to the surrounding nations.
options.note: The text explicitly calls Israel to be different from, not identical to, the surrounding nations.

quiz.q: How does Galatians 3:13 relate to the severity of judgment described in this chapter?

options.text: It says Christ redeemed us from the law's curse by becoming a curse for us, bearing the judgment sin deserves.
options.note: Galatians 3:13 states this directly.

options.text: It says the law's curse no longer has any meaning or seriousness at all.
options.note: Galatians 3:13 takes the curse seriously enough that Christ must bear it Himself, rather than dismissing its seriousness.

options.text: It has no connection to this chapter's themes.
options.note: Galatians 3:13 speaks directly to the curse and judgment this chapter's penalties describe.

options.text: It teaches that believers must still bear this judgment themselves.
options.note: Galatians 3:13 teaches the opposite: Christ bore the curse in believers' place.

questions: This chapter shows how seriously God regards sin against Himself and against the family He designed. Where have you grown casual about sin that God actually takes with this much weight?

The repeated phrase 'their blood is upon them' places responsibility for judgment on the guilty party. How does it change your view of the cross to realize Christ took that very judgment upon Himself in your place?
Israel was called to be set apart, holy, different from the nations around them. In what specific area of your life is God calling you to a costly distinctiveness right now?

The New Testament church responds to serious sin among believers not with execution but with loving discipline aimed at repentance and restoration. Is there someone in your life who needs that kind of firm, hopeful correction from you?

This chapter's severity can be hard to read. Where do you need to bring your discomfort honestly to God in prayer, rather than either dismissing the text or losing sight of the mercy that follows it in Christ?

translationNotes.note: The people of the land are to stone him indicates community, not individual, execution of this penalty, reflecting the corporate nature of covenant responsibility in Israel's judicial system.

Burned in the fire likely describes the method of execution itself, or possibly the burning of the body after execution by another means, a point ancient interpreters debated; either way it marks the offense as especially severe.
They shall die childless may describe a divinely imposed consequence rather than an additional human penalty, distinguishing this case from those explicitly assigned execution.
